items.hook: I can help with a range, but a gatehouse is not a fixed off-the-shelf item.
items.bridge: Span, total height, finish, and installation city can move the number a lot.
items.next_step: Ask for rough width, height, style, and city in DM.
items.handoff_suggestion: Route to Catcher if the user replies with project facts.

items.hook: That is already enough to start judging direction.
items.bridge: For a gatehouse, structure method, finish, and site access still shape the real range.
items.next_step: Ask for preferred material and a site photo.
items.handoff_suggestion: High-intent lead for Catcher and Abacus.

items.hook: Starting from style makes sense for this kind of project.
items.bridge: The right look changes a lot between a factory entrance, village gateway, scenic spot, and community gate.
items.next_step: Invite a site photo and one or two reference images.
items.handoff_suggestion: Mark as design-ready if the user shares references.

items.hook: Factory gatehouses usually need both image and traffic practicality.
items.bridge: Vehicle clearance, guard room relation, and brand signage all affect the layout.
items.next_step: Ask for entrance width, truck access, and logo needs.
items.handoff_suggestion: Route to Catcher when the use case is clearly factory entrance.

items.hook: Village gate projects often start with budget and image together, which is normal.
items.bridge: Usually the best first move is to lock the overall proportion and finish direction before chasing the lowest number.
items.next_step: Ask for the opening width and the look they want.

items.hook: There is rarely a one-size-fits-all answer on stone versus steel.
items.bridge: It depends on whether you care most about visual weight, timeline, maintenance, or budget.
items.next_step: Ask what scene the gatehouse is for and where it will be built.
items.handoff_suggestion: Lift lead value if the user keeps comparing methods.

items.hook: I get why the date matters here.
items.bridge: With gatehouse work, drawing finalization, fabrication, shipping, and site readiness all affect whether the date is realistic.
items.next_step: Ask for target date, city, rough size, and site status.

items.hook: Seeing direction first is a good way to avoid wrong turns.
items.bridge: Generic references help less than references matched to your actual proportions and scene.
items.next_step: Ask for the site photo and one reference style.

items.bridge: For gatehouse projects, dimensions, details, and how the job lands on site say more than a polished image alone.
items.next_step: Invite the user to name the exact point they doubt.

items.hook: Distance matters less than landing conditions here.
items.bridge: Transport, crane access, base readiness, and installation coordination are usually the real variables.
items.next_step: Ask for city, access limits, and target date.
items.handoff_suggestion: Human business follow-up if the user gives a real location.

items.hook: I can see why that would be frustrating.
items.bridge: The fastest way to judge a gatehouse issue is usually by the problem area, project timing, and a few clear photos.
items.next_step: Move the user to platform DM for photos and timing.

items.hook: A tight budget is workable if we cut the right parts, not the wrong ones.
items.bridge: For gatehouses, shape complexity, finish, and method can be traded off very differently.
items.next_step: Ask whether image, durability, or speed matters most.
items.handoff_suggestion: Keep as workable if the user engages on trade-offs.

items.hook: Happy to move faster on this.
items.bridge: The easiest first step is still to send the site photo, rough size, and city through platform DM so the direction is clear.
items.next_step: Use platform DM instead of public off-platform contact.

items.hook: Wanting to save demolition time is very normal.
items.bridge: Whether an old gatehouse can be wrapped or upgraded depends heavily on the current structure and base condition.
items.next_step: Ask for front, side, and detail photos of the existing gatehouse.
items.handoff_suggestion: Needs human review before strong commitment.

items.hook: That is the right kind of question to ask early.
items.bridge: For a gatehouse, span, wind exposure, base condition, and local environment all matter together.
items.next_step: Ask for city, rough size, and site condition, then escalate.
